      inconsistent_interface = 1
      program_error          = 2
      OTHERS                 = 3.

  IF sy-subrc EQ 0.

********This will create internal table which will have fields as per the Excel Format.
    CALL METHOD cl_alv_table_create=>create_dynamic_table
      EXPORTING
        it_fieldcatalog           = li_fieldcat
        i_length_in_byte          = 'X'
      IMPORTING
        ep_table                  = dy_table
      EXCEPTIONS
        generate_subpool_dir_full = 1
        OTHERS                    = 2.
    IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
      ASSIGN dy_table->* TO <dy_table>.
    ENDIF.

******V_col will hold the number of Columns inside Excel File which is later passed to ALSM_EXCEL_TO_INTERNAL_TABLE’ .

    DESCRIBE TABLE li_fieldcat LINES v_col.

    LOOP AT t_file INTO wa_file.

******This Block will create new area to store column wise data.<wa_temp> acts as a work area which is completely blank at start of new row
      AT NEW row.
        CREATE DATA new_line2 LIKE LINE OF <dy_table>.
        ASSIGN new_line2->* TO <wa_temp>.
      ENDAT.

*******for every iteration, wa_file-col will have increment till number of columns in Excel file and then reset to 1 when there’s new row i.e row = 1. (Refer example at end of blog)

      ASSIGN COMPONENT wa_file-col OF STRUCTURE <wa_temp> TO <fs_current_field>.
      IF sy-subrc EQ 0.

        <fs_current_field> = wa_file-value.
        UNASSIGN <fs_current_field>.
      ENDIF.

*****Finally once all Columns are processed and new row is about to start, append the workarea into Internal table

      AT END OF row.
        APPEND <wa_temp> TO it_xls_data.
        UNASSIGN <fs_current_field>.
        UNASSIGN <wa_temp>.
      ENDAT.

    ENDLOOP.
*ENDIF.

***Mapping IV_DATA_REF (Excel data) to Fields

*-----------------------FIELD SYMBOLS----------------------------------*
    FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lfs_data_tab> TYPE ANY TABLE,
                   <fs_1>         TYPE any,
                   <fs_2>         TYPE any.
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
    hdr_len = COND #( WHEN hdr_len IS INITIAL THEN 1 ELSE hdr_len ).

    ASSIGN iv_data_ref->* TO <lfs_data_tab>.

    IF <lfs_data_tab> IS ASSIGNED AND <dy_table> IS ASSIGNED.

      LOOP AT <lfs_data_tab> ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_data>).
        IF sy-tabix LE hdr_len.
          CONTINUE.  "Skip line 1
        ENDIF.

        CREATE DATA new_line2 LIKE LINE OF <dy_table>.
        ASSIGN new_line2->* TO <wa_temp>.

        DO 250 TIMES.
          ASSIGN COMPONENT sy-index OF STRUCTURE <lfs_data> TO <fs_1>.
          ASSIGN COMPONENT sy-index OF STRUCTURE <wa_temp> TO <fs_2>.
          READ TABLE li_fieldcat INTO DATA(fs_fieldcat) INDEX sy-index .
          IF sy-subrc IS INITIAL AND fs_fieldcat-datatype = 'DATS'.
            IF <fs_1> IS ASSIGNED AND <fs_2> IS ASSIGNED AND strlen( <fs_1> ) EQ 10 .
              DATA(lv_date) = |{ <fs_1>(4) }{ <fs_1>+5(2) }{ <fs_1>+8(2) }| .
              CONDENSE lv_date.
              <fs_2> = lv_date.
*              <fs_2> = <fs_1>.
              UNASSIGN: <fs_1>, <fs_2>.
              CLEAR: lv_date.
            ENDIF.
          ELSE.
            IF <fs_1> IS ASSIGNED AND <fs_2> IS ASSIGNED.
              <fs_2> = <fs_1>.
              UNASSIGN: <fs_1>, <fs_2>.
            ENDIF.
          ENDIF.
        ENDDO.

        IF <wa_temp> IS ASSIGNED AND <wa_temp> IS NOT INITIAL.
*          MOVE-CORRESPONDING <wa_temp> TO it_xls_data.
          APPEND <wa_temp> TO it_xls_data.
        ENDIF.

      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.

ENDFUNCTION.

⚠️ Important Points to Remember

  • ❌ Do NOT use BLKNR for text key

  • ✅ Always use ZEILE

  • ❌ Avoid INSERT = 'X'

  • ✅ Use INSERT = SPACE for overwrite

  • ✅ Ensure ESLZ entries exist

🧪 Debug Tip

Use function module READ_TEXT:

OBJECT = 'ESLZ'
ID     = 'LTXT'
NAME   = '001200400000000004'

👉 Helps verify actual stored text

Final Result

✔ All lines updated correctly
✔ Correct mapping in ML03
✔ No missing or overwritten data

 Conclusion

Uploading Service Catalog Long Text becomes easy once you understand:

  • SAP stores text per LINE (ZEILE)

  • Not per BLOCK (BLKNR)

  • Correct text key is the key to success

💡 Key Takeaway

If your long text upload is not working — you are most likely using the wrong key.

🙌 Closing Note

This is a common issue with no clear documentation.
Hope this blog saves you hours of debugging.

Feel free to share your feedback or improvements 🚀

entry.title: Create, Delete and Updating of Packing Specification(PACKSPEC) through Upload Program Report
entry.content:
Introduction:

Maintaining of Packing Specification(Minimum Order Quantity and Target Quantity) for every Material-Plant combination in Service Parts Planning(SPP) system is a crucial step. Usually we do that through Standard T-Code /SCWM/IPU through upload file.

Instead to improve user experience and simplify the process of uploading, we created an Upload Report Program.

Body:

Development Completed on April 4 , 2025

Usually, creating, deleting or updating Packspec in T-Code /SCWM/PACKSPEC is a time-consuming process and requires lot of manual efforts to change every packspec directly in the standard screen.

This can also be done in T-Code /SCWM/IPU using upload template, but that is not user-friendly which requires us to download standard template of more than 100 columns and wants us to edit each and every column and upload. This is quite difficult.

It will also create a new packspec every time in the system, which will also increase the count of No. of Packspec in system, and which is also not consolidated with respect to Minimum Quantity-Target Quantity or plant-material combo. This makes us to go for custom upload program which is quite efficient in analyzing the Minimum Quantity-Target Quantity, Plant-material combination and decides to craete/update packspec based on user input.

Our Requirement is to reduce the manual intervention of changing the packspec directly in standard screen or reduce the complexity structure of upload file, instead we can have an upload program which does the Create, Update or Delete processes and provide results of the progress in SLG1 log and also as ALV report for end-users.

General Overview about Packing Specification:

One Packspec number consists of many condition records. Each Condition record represents one product and one location. 

Business users usually maintains set of material-plant combo under one specific packspec number with MOQ(Minimum Order Quantity)(Minimum Quantity for Supplier - SAP system identification name) and UOQ(Unit Order Quantity)( Target Quantity - SAP system identification name).

They will also be changing this from time-to-time as per business changes.

Detailed Overview about our Report Prorgam:

Requirement consists of 2 mode namely Manual Mode and Full Mode.

  1. Manual Mode is to change only the MOQ and UOQ of the packspec number, instead of keeping hands on the condition records. Suppose if business wants to change MOQ. UOQ from 2,3 to 6,2 respectively for all the same set of mat-loc combo present in the packspec number, they can use this manual mode to change only the MOQ and UOQ.
  2. Full Mode will consume upload file as input. Upload Template:
    MaterialPlantDEPL/DRPMOQUOQMode A/D
  3. From inputs from this file, it will check mode first as A/D.
  4. Based on that, it checks if the material-loc combo is present under any other packspec number, if yes, program will delete existing condition record and create a new one with given MOQ and UOQ.
  5. Above is example of one scenario, like this it should work for every possible scenario.
  6. Delete a packspec , if suppose the last condition record in packspec is requested to be deleted.
  7. Create a new packspec if a new set of MOQ-UOQ is requested n upload file.
  8. Add one more condition record, if the requested MOQ-UOQ is already present in System.
Arriving at Solution:
  1. We need to debug the standard T-code /SCWM/IPU to find all the standard FM's used to create, update and delete Packspec . FM's used to create, change, delete CONDITION RECORD is also required.
  2. Function Modules found:
    • ‘/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_READ’ - To read the existing packspec details
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_CREATE' - To create a new Packspec
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_2ND_VERSION' - To create 2nd version of packspec (i.e) make it inactive, add required condition records.
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_CHANGE' - To change the MOQ and UOQ of the packspec
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_ACTIVATE' - To activate the inactive packspec.
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_DELETE' - To Delete the whole packspec
    • '/SCWM/API_CONDITION_REPLICATE' - To create only one condition record within pakspec
    • '/SCWM/PS_DELETE_CONDTECH' - To delete only condition record, if last condition record, call packspec delete FM also.
    • '/SAPCND/MNT_SAVE_OW' - After deleting the condition record, call this FM to make the deletion process complete...as this is the process followed in standard process for successful deletion.
    • 'OWN_LOGICAL_SYSTEM_GET' - To get the logical system number.
  3. Tables used:
    • '/1CN/CPSCUS_SPP' - To get packspec details of Deployment Packspec.
    • '/1CN/CPSCUS_PRPL ' - To get packspec details of DRP Packspec.
    • '/SCWM/PNPAKH' - Pack Spec Header(ippe node)
    • 'PALTID' - iPPE Alternative: Time-Independent ID and Attributes
    • '/SCWM/PAPAK' - packaging specification level (ippe alternative)
  4. We created a report named "ZSPPR_PACKSPEC_UPLOAD" with selection screen to run the program and a backend global class named "ZCL_PS_MASTER" to support the required logics needed.
  5. ZSPPR_PACKSPEC_UPLOADZCL_PS_MASTER global class
  6. Report consists of Top declarations, Selection screen logics including download the template of upload file, Modification ID to differentiate between manual and full mode , Program flow with object for ZCL_PS_MASTER and flow logic and forms with different set of operations involved like change_moq_uoq, upload_data_from excel_to_itab, data_validation, refresh_depl_table, refresh_drp_table, add_mode, delete_mode, Write_log, calculate_pass_fail records for summary, display manual ALV and display Full ALV output as result.
  7. Importing data from Excel to ITAB can be done using Standard FM like CL_GUI_FRONTEND_SERVICES=>GUI_UPLOAD, but this method has errors of terminating the whole SAP GUI session while trying to close the opened Excel file by our program.
  8. To resolve this we came up with solution of importing set of custom classes ‘ZCL_EXCEL_READER_2007' into a separate package. This is available in abap2xlsx github repository. GitHub - abap2xlsx/abap2xlsx: Generate your professional Excel spreadsheet from ABAP · GitHub 
  9. With help of ABAPGIT Standalone program , we were able to add this set of classes into a new package.  Installation | abapGit Docs 
  10. Using method GET_CELL of class ‘ZCL_EXCEL_READER_2007' , we will get all the data in excel to ITAB, without popping up of Excel GUI explicitly, avoiding unwanted termination of SAP GUI.
  11. Once these classes are installed, we can make use multiple times in our future upload programs.
  12. ALV display is enhanced with colored outputs with green/red for users to identify the error records easily.
  13. The below logic is used to identify that given line of Packspec comes under which condition of Add/Delete Packspec.
    • [MODE - A]
    • IF  MOQ-UOQ from excel is present in system.
    •       IF MAT-LOC from excel is in the same MOQ-UOQ from EXCEL.
    •                   Throw ERROR"Same packspec combination already exists.".
    •       ELSEIF (Condition Record is present in system in another MOQ-UOQ).
    •                   Delete that Condition Record from existing packspec, create new condition record under given MOQ-UOQ.     
    •       ELSE.
    •                   Add the MAT-LOC to the given packspec.
    •       ENDIF.
    • ELSE.
    •       IF Condition Record is present in system.
    •                   Delete that Condition Record from existing packspec, create a new PACKSPEC.
    •       ELSE.
    •                   Create a new PACKSPEC.                                                                         
    • ENDIF.
    • [MODE - D].
    • IF MOQ-EOQ from excel is present in system.
    •       IF MAT-LOC from excel is in the same MOQ-EOQ from EXCEL.
    •                   IF No. of Condition Records = 1
    •                               Delete the PACKSPEC.
    •                   ELSE.
    •                               Delete the Condition Record from PACKSPEC.
    •                   ENDIF.
    •       ELSE.
    •                   Condition record is not under the given MOQ-UOQ.
    •       ENDIF.
    • ELSE.
    •       Packspec(MOQ-UOQ) does not exist in system-THROW error.
    • ENDIF.
  14. Technical Mindmap for the program flow:
  16.  Screenshots of Manual Mode Test run ALV, Real run ALV and SLG1 log output.
  17. Test Run ALV OutputReal Run ALV OutputReal Run SLG1 log Output

    Screenshots of Full mode Test run ALV, Real run ALV and SLG1 log output.

    Test Run ALV OutputReal Run ALV OutputReal Run SLG1 Log output

    Validation check results Screenshot:

  18. Difficult part is to find the relationship between the tables , where the condition number is getting stored.
  19. While debugging, the condition record actually gets saved after another FM call of MNT_SAVE_OW, but to find that exact FM took really more time.
  20. Faced lot of difficulties in DB storage malfunction due to continuous execution of FM's, so found solution with COMMIT WORK and Wait upto 2 seconds. This resolved the DB overflow issue.
  21. Learned how to stop debugger exactly at CALL FUNCTION points while executing a t-code. 
  22. You can create SQVI query like below for your convenience at the time of development for testing purpose and data-validation!
  23.  SQVI query for Deployment packspecSQVI query for DRP packspec

Conclusion:

We can create upload reports in this way consisting of Minimum and Target Quantity with Plant-Location Combination with mode A/D. We can modify this according to business use-cases and include /exclude columns in upload file. We've kept changing parameters in TVARVC table and hard-coded the constant parameters.

This was developed when I had 8-9 months of experience, excuse me for best coding practices. Attached complete code for your reference! Please feel free to post your comments and message me for any support regarding this! Hope this helps someone! 😊

entry.title: BP Extension - Search By Email in ECC 6.0
entry.content:
The Client was having a legacy system where they can search the for particular Vendor if its created or not based on email ID. So moving forward they came up with requirement to centralize the Vendor Management System by migrating all things into ECC.

The first requirement came to make BP screen as it was in legacy system where user can search for Partner numbers created in system based on Email Id provided. I went through many SAP notes and found multiple approaches.

Approach

  • SPRO config – Search Criteria
SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Logistics - General → Cross-Application Components → SAP Business Partner → Business Partner Search → Define Search Criteria for Business Partner



OR

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Logistics - General → Cross-Application Components → SAP Business Partner → Business Partner Search → Basic Setting → Search → Define Search Views

Note: These will only work if the BP Search Framework is fully active

  • BUS config

  • LOCA_CUST

The above 2 options didn’t worked due to lower SAP version. So the only option was to explore LOCA_CUST.

Here is the details of all technical objects created and mapping to LOCA_CUST.

  1. Create a Database View (SE11)
    Since email addresses live in table ADR6, we need a view that joins the BP to its email.
     Tables & Joins:
     BUT000 (Partner) joined to BUT020 (BP/Address Link) on PARTNER = PARTNER.
     BUT020 joined to ADR6 (Email) on ADDRNUMBER = ADDRNUMBER.

     

  2. Create an Elementary Search Help
    Once the view is active, create the search help that the BP transaction will actually display.
    SE11 -> Search Help
     Selection Method: Enter the view you just created (ZBP_EMAIL_SEARCH).
     Search Help Exit: Use F4UT_OPTIMIZE_COLWIDTH (optional, for better UI).
     Parameters:
     SMTP_ADDR: Mark as IMP (Import) and EXP (Export). LPos = 1, SPos = 1.


  3. Append to the Collective Search Help
    Now you must tell the BP transaction to include your new "Email" tab in the locator.
    Open Collective Search Help BUPA_ALL
    Go to the Included Search Helps tab.
    Click the Append Search Help button (F5).
    Create a new Append . In the "Included Search Helps" of your append, add your elementary search help

    Click Parameter Assignment to map the PARTNER field from your search help to the collective search help.



  4.  Verification
    After activating everything:
    Run transaction BP.
    In the Locator (left-hand side), click the "Find By" dropdown.
    You should now see your new entry ( with the Z name).

    NOTE: However if you click on the new field it will throw error “Program name and Screen are not configured”     

  5.  Pass the value of email field in Standard SAP flow of BP transaction
    Create Append structure in BUS_JOEL_SEARCH. SE11 ->  BUS_JOEL_SEARCH - >Append Structure(F5)


  6. Here comes the Important Step for mapping to SAP hook using ABAP code. This FM will capture the data of new field and integrate it into the standard BP process for this we need to use the OOPS concept and include some standard processing code from LBUPA_DIALOG_SEARCHF02 and LBUS_LOCATORO01.
  7. Create a Function Group. SE37-> Goto-> Function Group -> Create Group .


  8. Click on Master Program of function group and define a local class in TOP include along with the Global variable for fetching the value of email in GV_EMAIL.
FUNCTION-POOL ZBP_FMGRP_BUPA.               "MESSAGE-ID ..

* INCLUDE LZBP_FMGRP_BUPAD...                " Local class definition
TABLES bus_joel_search.

DATA: gs_dynpro_id TYPE bus_screen-area,
      gv_email type adr6-smtp_addr. " capture screen value

CLASS lcl_searcher DEFINITION FINAL.

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    CLASS-METHODS:
    select_by_email
    IMPORTING iv_search  TYPE REF TO cl_bus_locator_search
              iv_email TYPE bus_joel_search-smtp_addr
              EXCEPTIONS search_values_missing.

ENDCLASS.
  • Create a Copy FM from Standard BUPA_DIALOG_SEARCH into a  Z Function module. Follow the below code to fetch the partner from the view created in STEP1 based on input from email of BP screen.
FUNCTION zbp_bupa_dialog_emailrch.
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
*"*"Local Interface:
*"  IMPORTING
*"     REFERENCE(IV_SEARCH) TYPE REF TO  CL_BUS_LOCATOR_SEARCH
*"  EXCEPTIONS
*"      SEARCH_VALUES_MISSING
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------

* Local data.
  DATA: ls_search_fields TYPE bus_joel_search. 
* Convert generic container into specific fields.
  ls_search_fields = iv_search->gv_search_fields.           

* Choose the select.
  CASE iv_search->gv_search_id.

*   The empty search?
    WHEN space.
      CALL METHOD lcl_searcher=>select_by_nothing
                  EXPORTING iv_search = iv_search.

    WHEN 'ZBP_EMAIL_SRCHELP'.    "name of elementary search help
      CALL METHOD lcl_searcher=>select_by_email
        EXPORTING
          iv_search             = iv_search
          iv_email              = ls_search_fields-smtp_addr
        EXCEPTIONS
          search_values_missing = 1.

    WHEN OTHERS.
      CLEAR sy-subrc.
  ENDCASE.
*
  CASE sy-subrc.
    WHEN 1.
      RAISE search_values_missing.
  ENDCASE.

ENDFUNCTION.

​
  • Implement the method by creating a new include in Master Program.

    with the below code the Partner GUID is passed back again to BP framework to display the result of partner number based on the user input of email id. 

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&  Include           ZBP_FMGRP_BUPA01
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
CLASS lcl_searcher IMPLEMENTATION.
*---------------------------------------------------------------------
  METHOD select_by_email.
*   Local data.

    DATA: lv_partner_type       TYPE bus_partner-type,
          lr_email TYPE RANGE OF adr6-smtp_addr,
          lt_partner_guids  TYPE bus_partner-guid_table.


    IF gv_email NE space.
      SELECT partner_guid INTO TABLE lt_partner_guids
               FROM zbp_email_search
               UP TO iv_search->gv_maximum_rows ROWS
               WHERE smtp_addr EQ gv_email.
*      BREAK-POINT.

      IF sy-subrc NE 0.
        TRANSLATE gv_email TO LOWER CASE.
        SELECT partner_guid INTO TABLE lt_partner_guids
             FROM zbp_email_search
             UP TO iv_search->gv_maximum_rows ROWS
             WHERE smtp_addr EQ gv_email.
      ENDIF.

    ENDIF.
*   Pass result table
    CALL METHOD cl_bupa_dialog_searcher=>add_partner_guids_to_result
      EXPORTING
        it_partner_guids         = lt_partner_guids
        iv_filter_by_search_type = space
        iv_search                = iv_search.
  ENDMETHOD.                    "select_by_email

ENDCLASS.                    "lcl_searcher IMPLEMENTATION
  • Create a new screen by Right click on Function group -> Create->Screen

    Note: GV_EMAIL is the same which is declared in step 8.Don’t forget to make the text box as scrollable otherwise you may loose data .

  • Double click on PBO and PAI and create new include in Master Program of function group. And do the following code. This code helps to pass the necessary information of standard processing copied from LBUPA_DIALOG_SEARCHF02 and LBUS_LOCATORO01.
----------------------------------------------------------------------*
***INCLUDE LZBP_FMGRP_BUPAO01.
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  DYNPRO_PBO  OUTPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE dynpro_pbo OUTPUT.
  gs_dynpro_id-program_name = sy-repid.
  gs_dynpro_id-dynpro_number = sy-dynnr.

  CALL METHOD cl_bus_abstract_screen=>dynpro_pbo
              EXPORTING iv_program_name = gs_dynpro_id-program_name
                        iv_dynpro_number = gs_dynpro_id-dynpro_number.

ENDMODULE.                 " DYNPRO_PBO  OUTPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  DYNPRO_PAI  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE dynpro_pai INPUT.
  gs_dynpro_id-program_name = sy-repid.
  gs_dynpro_id-dynpro_number = sy-dynnr.

  CALL METHOD cl_bus_abstract_screen=>dynpro_pai
              EXPORTING iv_program_name = gs_dynpro_id-program_name
                        iv_dynpro_number = gs_dynpro_id-dynpro_number.
ENDMODULE.                 " DYNPRO_PAI  INPUT
​
  • Create a new include in Master Program of function group and enter the below code. These subroutines are called from the BUS_JEOL_LOCATOR framework hence it is required in our FM.
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       FORM bus_locator_get_search_fields                            *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*

FORM bus_locator_get_search_fields                          "#EC CALLED
     USING    iv_search_application TYPE bus_locator-search_application
     CHANGING ev_search_fields      TYPE bus_locator-search_fields.

 CASE iv_search_application.
    WHEN cl_bupa_dialog_searcher=>gc_search_application_bupa.
      ev_search_fields = bus_joel_search."#EC ENHOK
  ENDCASE.

ENDFORM.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       FORM bus_locator_set_search_fields                            *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*

FORM bus_locator_set_search_fields                          "#EC CALLED
     USING    iv_search_application TYPE bus_locator-search_application
              iv_search_fields      TYPE bus_locator-search_fields.

  CASE iv_search_application.
    WHEN cl_bupa_dialog_searcher=>gc_search_application_bupa.
      bus_joel_search = iv_search_fields. "#EC ENHOK
  ENDCASE.

ENDFORM.
  • Functional Configuration in LOCA_CUST

    Create new entry under Search by ID

    Search for the Search Help created in Step gy doing an F4 on Locator Search ID.

    Screen text would be displayed on the drop of Find by screen of BP .Now do F4 for FM and Program name and enter screen number created in step 11 the details. That’s the end for the required config for mapping the new field to BP screen.



  • Let’s test
    Enter Tcode BP. Click on BY dropdown to see the new field available under name Email.

    Enter any relevant email Id to find the respective BP . You can find the BP from the view created in Step 1 as well.

    Result

entry.title: SPP SNC (Supply Portal Collaboration) Portal - BADI - Web Dynpro Screen EPN Column inclusion
entry.content:
Introduction:

When we need to modify an existing column in SNC(Supply Network Collaboration) Portal under any tab with Header name and column values, we need to follow follow several set of procedures to find correct BADI and implement it.

In this blog I've explained about adding column EPN(Engineering Part Number) under Release Process->Overview tab in SNC portal.

Body:

Development Completed on Feb 5 , 2026
  1. Business requested to include EPN(Engineering Part Number) column in the SAP SNC(Supply Network Collaboration) Portal.
  2. T-Code for SNC Portal under APO box is /SAPAPO/SPP_CUSTOMER or we can also use /SAPAPO/SPP_SUPPLIER
  3. SNC Poral Initial Screen
  4. Under this screen go to Release Process->Overview Tab.
  5. Here you can see 7th column named as "My Product Number" should be changed to "Eng. Part Number (EPN)" column with the necessary EPN numbers to be populated against every Product number.
Arriving at Solution:
  1. After going to Release Process->Overview tab, go to debugging screen and need to find what are the available and useful BADI's for this solution to enhance this standard Web Dynpro screen.
  2. To trigger debugging WD screen within SAP system, go to T-Code /N/SCF/WD_START and provide our Application ID : /SCF/SPP_CUSTOMER and check the checkbox "Internal Modus". So that it Web Dynpro Screen will start within SAP system. We can set breakpoint at "CALL BADI" statement and find the available BADI's.
  3. Other way to find the class involved in particular Web Dynpro Screen is go to T-Code "/N/SCF/SCREENCFG" and click on ICH and go to screen definitions, then click on DSOVW-Release process Overview Screen (Supplier View) and click on Screen Layout. You will get the classes involved in fetching data etc.
  4. This process is same for all Web Dynpro screens to get the respective class name.
  5. With help of class name, we can get the logics involved in populating the screen data and BADI's called in-between.
  6. We got BADI "/SCF/RELEASE_UI" which is useful here to populate the data from the backend. Create Enhancement Implementation and one BADI implementation within the created Enhancement Implementation.
  7. BADI 1:
    BADI Definition Name/SCF/RELEASE_UI
    Interface Used/SCF/IF_EX_RELEASE_UI
    Class where BADI is Triggered /SCF/CL_MDLTBV_DSOVERVIEW
    Method called in ClassLOAD_DATA
    Enhancement ImplementationZ*
    BADI ImplementationZ*
    Implementing ClassZ*
    Method used/SCF/IF_EX_RELEASE_UI~LOAD_OVERVIEW_TBV
  8. Always keep your enhancement codes within Enhancement Switch which is maintained in a separate custom table. So that, whenever there's a misfortune happens, we can turn it off immediately to prevent more damages. Always check the Switch turned on and write your own logic.
  9. In this BADI code logic, we are fetching all the EPN values from Transparent Table /SAPAPO/MATAPN and assigning against the column "CUSTMATERIAL "(My Product Number column).
  10. This BADI will push the data into the target column.
  11. Next, we need another BADI to change the Column header name in Web Dynpro Screen.
  12. We got BADI "WD_BADI_DOMODIFYVIEW" which will be triggered multiple times. Hence, we have filter option where we need to fill required parameters.
  13. BADI 2:
    BADI Definition NameWD_BADI_DOMODIFYVIEW
    Interface UsedIF_WD_BADI_DOMODIFYVIEW
    Class where BADI is Triggered CL_WDR_DELEGATING_VIEW
    Method called in ClassDO_MODIFY_VIEW
    Enhancement ImplementationZ*
    BADI ImplementationZ*
    Implementing ClassZ*
    Method usedIF_WD_BADI_DOMODIFYVIEW~WDDOMODIFYVIEW
  14. Here we are using COMPONENT_NAME as "SALV_WD_TABLE" and VIEW_NAME as "VIEW_TABLE".{ These BADI's aren't that easy to find like I mentioned here simply, it takes lot of effort to find each and every BADI 😊}. You can also find the exact component and view name from technical help on the WD screen.
  15.  
  16. These parameters we need to find through debugging as and when our logic needs to be executed.
  17. Create EI and BI like previous BADI and add the filter values.
  18. In the BADI code logic, check for Enhancement Switch and get the exact object where our Column Header name lies within. We need to change both Header name and Caption name (visible during Hovering the column header). 
  19. We can compare ID of both elements, but it keeps on changing from system to system, but name remains consistent. Hence, We choose name of the column.
  20. To find the element name from the WD screen, right click on the column header name from WD screen and press "Technical Help". Go to "View and View Elements" tab. Now, dropdown the "View Elements and Properties" tab. Now the dropped down screen exactly represents the properties of column header where you right clicked for technical help.
  22. Catch correct exceptions in correct order where subclass exception must be caught first, and then superclass exception can be caught. CX_ROOT must be caught at last of all exceptions. This BADI will also be triggered in other screens of SNC portal also, hence, if we miss catching exception, it will result in Screen Dump.
  23. I faced lot of difficulties while finding correct component name, view name, tooltip, text of target elements. Biggest difficulty happened was to find the class name from where the data on screen is getting populated from. 
  24. Previous State/when Enhancement Switch is turned off :

  25.  

    Current State/when Enhancement Switch is turned On:

Conclusion:

This is how we change column name and related contents in SNC Portal. I've also worked on adding column "Commitment Level" column under "Receipts and Requirements"->"Due list for Purchasing Documents" tab by implementing the BADI "/SCF/ICH_POWL". Attached complete code for your reference! Please feel free to post your comments and message me for any support regarding this. Hope this helps someone! 😊

entry.title: Fiori List Report: The "Process All" Dilemma — Parsing OData $filter in ABAP
entry.content:
The Problem

This scenario is more common than you might think.

In the old SAP GUI world, ALV lists frequently had buttons like "Execute All" or "Process All" defined in the GUI Status, or users could simply "Select All" rows and trigger an action. The entire dataset was right there in memory — stateful, accessible, simple.

Unfortunately, the same approach is still often requested in Fiori Elements List Reports. The reason? There's still a widespread difficulty in understanding the fundamental difference between stateful and stateless, between a classic ALV list and a Fiori List Report.

When we try to explain that there's an OData protocol with pagination behind the scenes, that "Select All" doesn't truly select all rows, or that an "Execute All" action has no knowledge of which records to process — unlike in ALV where the entire internal table was available — we can often steer the design away from this pattern. But sometimes, we can't.

The Fiori warning message helps users understand that "Select All" is only partial — it's no longer the same as it was with ALV lists.

There are legitimate use cases where, after filtering a dataset, reviewing it, validating it, the user needs to "confirm" an action or a status change to be applied to that specific result set they're currently looking at.

I won't go into the details of how the action should be implemented in ABAP — there could be timeout concerns, and often these actions are executed via background tasks or jobs. I want to focus specifically on the approach for capturing the "snapshot" of what the user has filtered.

As we said, "Select All" is not a viable option. In the modern world we have pagination, and we can't expect the user to scroll all the way to the bottom of the list. Retrieving all row identifiers from the frontend is simply not feasible.

Our Approach

The best solution we've implemented (together with @SimoneLicciardi ) is to pass the applied filter criteria back to a Static Action — essentially the same filters that were active when the user pressed "Go".

We can define an Abstract Entity as the import parameter for the RAP action. If the action dialog already has other parameters, this is simply an additional hidden parameter (@UI.hidden: true) that we can call FilterString.

define abstract entity ZA_ProcessAllParams {
  @UI.hidden: true
  FilterString : abap.string(4000);
  // ... other action parameters if needed
}

The Fiori Elements Extension

On the Fiori Elements List Report side, we need custom action logic that does two things when the user triggers the action: capture the current $filter string and inject it into the hidden parameter.

This can be achieved through a custom action defined in the manifest.json (or via a controller extension, depending on your app setup). 

The _getFilterString method extracts the $filter from the current table row binding — this is exactly the same filter the framework sent to the backend when the user pressed "Go".

function _getFilterString(oView) {
    // Table ID follows Fiori Elements convention: 
    // "<appId>::<ListReportPage>--fe::table::<EntitySet>::LineItem"
    var oTable = oView.byId("<your-table-control-id>");
    if (oTable) {
        var oRowBinding = oTable.getRowBinding();
        if (oRowBinding) {
            var sPath = oRowBinding.getDownloadUrl();
            if (sPath) {
                var iFilterStart = sPath.indexOf("$filter=");
                if (iFilterStart > -1) {
                    var sFilter = sPath.substring(iFilterStart + 8);
                    var iNextParam = sFilter.indexOf("&");
                    if (iNextParam > -1) {
                        sFilter = sFilter.substring(0, iNextParam);
                    }
                    return decodeURIComponent(sFilter);
                }
            }
        }
    }
    return "";
}

Then in the action handler, we invoke the static action and pass the $filter string as the hidden parameter:

onPressExecuteAll: function () {
    var oView = this.editFlow.getView();
    var sActionName = "<your action bound context>";
    var sFilterString = _getFilterString(oView);

    var oInvokeParams = {
        controlId: "<your-table-control-id>",
        bStaticAction: true,
        invocationGrouping: "Isolated",
        parameterValues: [{
            name: "FilterString",
            value: sFilterString
        }]
    };

    this.editFlow.invokeAction(sActionName, oInvokeParams)
        .then(function (oResult) {
            // handle success
        }).catch(function (oError) {
            console.error("Action error:", oError);
        });
}

The key point: the parameterValues array injects the filter string into the hidden FilterString field of the Abstract Entity. The user never sees it — the action dialog shows only the visible parameters (if any), while the filter criteria travel silently to the backend.

Note:  this was tested on S/4HANA 2023 with OData V4 and SAPUI5 1.120.

The ABAP Parser

On the backend, the action handler receives the $filter string and needs to parse it into ABAP range tables to re-execute the query with the same criteria.

We looked for standard solutions — for example, the method ODATA_FILTER2SELECT_OPTION of class CL_CLB2_TOOLS — but it only supports OData V2.

So we built a custom reusable class: ZCL_ODATA_FILTER_PARSER, which handles both V2 and V4 syntax and converts the $filter string directly into ABAP range tables.

DATA(lo_parser) = NEW zcl_odata_filter_parser(
  iv_filter  = lv_filter_string
  iv_version = zcl_odata_filter_parser=>gc_version-v4
).

DATA(lt_r_plant) = lo_parser->get_range( 'Plant' ).
DATA(lt_r_mat)   = lo_parser->get_range( 'Material' ).
DATA(lt_r_date)  = lo_parser->get_range( 'DeliveryDate' ).

The class is open source, includes ABAP Unit tests  and is installable via abapGit. You can find the  usage examples in the repository:

https://github.com/alespad/abap-odata-filter-parser 

This is how we approached solving this problem. If you know of better approaches, closer to standard functionality, we'd love to hear about them — please share in the comments!

entry.title: Send Email with inline BASE64 Image in SAP ABAP.
entry.content:
Introduction 

I recently came across the need to send an automatically generated email based on a given HTML template. To my surprise, this time the template arrived with embedded/inline (src="data:image/png;base64,...) images using Base64 encoding. I should mention that all previous HTML mail templates I worked with contained image links pointing to a content web server.

Since this was new to me, I did some research on the topic but did not find much information, except for this helpful blog post:
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/send-email-with-image-from-mime-repository-in-sap-abap/ba-p/14285931 

In that scenario, the images are stored in the MIME repository, whereas in my case they are embedded directly in the HTML file. Therefore, I needed to extract the picture content from the HTML code, rebuild the template, and generate an email using standard SAP BCS functionality.

After implementing this successfully, I would like to share some of my coding approach with the community.

Code Example: 

REPORT zemail_to_send_img2.

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Data Declarations
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
TYPES: BEGIN OF lty_pic_str,
         name TYPE string,
         mime TYPE w3conttype,
         src  TYPE xstring,
       END OF lty_pic_str.

DATA: lv_html_in  TYPE string,               "HTML body string (input)
      lv_html_out TYPE string,               "HTML body string (output)
      lt_html_out TYPE soli_tab,             "HTML body lines
      lv_pic_cnt  TYPE i,                    "Picture counter
      lv_src      TYPE xstring,              "Image content in XSTRING
      lt_solix    TYPE solix_tab,            "Binary table
      lv_len      TYPE so_obj_len,           "Length of binary content
      lt_pic_tab  TYPE TABLE OF lty_pic_str. "Table with pictures

DATA: lo_mime_srv  TYPE REF TO cl_gbt_multirelated_service,
      lo_doc       TYPE REF TO cl_document_bcs,
      lo_bcs       TYPE REF TO cl_bcs,
      lo_sender    TYPE REF TO if_sender_bcs,
      lo_recipient TYPE REF TO if_recipient_bcs.

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 1: Build HTML Email Body
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
lv_html_in =
   |<html>|
&& |<!-- To work with BCS, styles should be defined locally !!! -->|
&& |<body style="background-color:#e6f0fa; font-family:Arial; margin:0; padding:10px;">|
&& |<div style="max-width:600px; margin:auto; background:#ffffff; padding:20px; border-radius:8px; border:1px solid #d0d7e3;">|
&& | <div style="text-align:center; margin-bottom:15px;">|
&& |<!-- Inline BASE64 PNG Image -->|
&& | <img src="data:image/png;base64,|
&& |iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAKMAAACUCAMAAADIzWmnAAAAY1BMVEX///8AAABHR0f7+/vz8/PV1dXw8PA6Ojr29vbk5OTp6ekyMj|
&& |Ls7OwpKSnc3NxAQECcnJyRkZFfX1/MzMxkZGStra2+vr5WVlZQUFB1dXUeHh6mpqaLi4sPDw99fX1vb28XFxcA3zptAAAMl0lEQVR4|
&& |nO1c52LyvA6G7EH2hCzu/yo/Sw7BQ06B0vGe0+dPm0Esy1qWZR8Of/jDH/7wh/97RG7bDnkxAop8aCs3+mmSRERtUVp+cpmOd0yXxL|
&& |fqovoFhAZpO/vHPfhjmwY/SGGaL7FGVKKTuQzhzxDoNOVFpOSCV6UbuOX9cnu4tN/PTLeQaDiWOZK24MMFic076Y04d7+Xwlke0a71|
&& |DhX723MViXr2f3XwWoXKOf0+Csu1zZJLYwkMOjHVuVTrGxVjsn+Cf/irVr3K7fI9VKbzlbc7tAtqRIOSNrB/x+2lkV0N8I/TAHXXuR|
&& |osLpjj16uPPXBT4zdehDwqeZseXHjbax484ZcpDvjZ8Rr+y6z5Ygpd3k7Zsrbhn+utwZZdFMKbBbtu1/8HFAw29C0f+f5LB3zEYU4a|
&& |hwllBg3fyDiw1hNRb12mVOXtogEN60IYedS1ZPgyCl0uUgWMoQti5m9URTCctvCufWZ3NkeIb1tw6RVHQULeDs6DGnU3hDHP7g0Nwt|
&& |BytDetQaTwfocCWtUoz/Lb74GdIwMGBy48aCcTxIpdXzzpfY+Zn/p+iZyckdMOyuf0/vE+Ye87Prj2DFZECGmAgrPyCzbYsSCh4eWu|
&& |VS6q+uwd3oqQf9XhV8CIpBIeN+hXZIDfEc1Me7nLgzOj2LyVyBSc2zFfwwLQWal54GusRopRfBvcFdAzfxXhACWnfGN06UL0er0xKg|
&& |Krk4vPvatCzo3wq8QpcD7lOhSHCsxY8jb1RsPtb2OJTUmRlivp8A3ANynUcXqxcxV8tX8TkWg34k2JQc4SOcwaBC7fAZySKXdBJDeq|
&& |UjSabyEyTCRT6PWKMB7QyfQn7YenXnA1HMDaehOKMBMk9DPAYOGY5Tc+QjOd/ErAerGo4sgEkoVFiRJ9W4KtT3kotHw6QA+W44ppbs|
&& |MAzdxV6TqoFGWRWXcmJfaGQIQFlkHUrhHekdC2ZwHudZuTXur8TKiHphw32nWpOIDNmfNaCuM/6XFaRt/UVOer8MnrWEVS10HRKV7Y|
&& |RzHshRtRNYuT8OtSNZMQvr8E9LLYzWouxWnq1M9566ahF9gHh0lsRv48Q3toB16YulV+7kX64m6uoGOD4vmfBijesv5vu82YHSVMfr|
&& |fMRcGIL9M0jLwgsKFZ2w4CLwrTlBEfF8W8dNkk/zAbG/emKouuhM8AdVj0VzCq2UVpEKlNkjj2szv8OE4S6sUr9FOM2KOOENuHAUb4|
&& |KioDzFI655C2+Vxbej5iH4lVM/lI0cBeRHvjsmYuL869A7CMkl8GpeRKaDuhW7XDyIbxI1qTrFtGyKOFjr19pVU/S9jXRwAjLfllh9|
&& |lcXwlV7MCpYZY45ON8XpaaY1nO85gPMBOsnUBpPmTy24n3kBkvjTZYa9kvgyEq9BctIxfA01j6bSbVk8RICPYS3Zl+DNAPybodIDDV|
&& |vSt4QoOrgPBM9YYH7mzkvo7anYcA6ZBJuhMmWoyAt49mTzGQvTp0WigxabHUI9CdXkNLjauOnACQDqLpRtUa7Mz8LIkwHp0832A2wy|
&& |ccQrPjzGAwiG5BRFpLd7yO5vguzlpXT4ausqgjMfmyNCHlDBMEspy2zzMSPm7pX6EMBNNd3zTB8/y7LxWhzyMD6+nZTaETxPo+UfO4|
&& |3hRRAJjj64nb4aRxrVEdxkeIWP9jmY0Q+RO2Ds0jRQbH3o9k5gfMslvP2MhW7xSIP9VPJ1PlXwTzQZlD3M91W5Nr47+PUtcD+ARlwc|
&& |Czmc0vk5mYkjIiRE9p82sCZurkoQZVzKihAD9m9rWNwTafMs05BaDsj2dXCGvtxBrZHCAD5jxdazCeQJCvCIHBR9Cw2VBflA8YPR64|
&& |ErMYVSYnBI5FsRLORZx8fwDdDXAtIhuD3punI6mJNy2hIbWUdt0HZa1HOZt4hyFsWGFkv0uomu7GzYD4Tm221gzaCqbvV7NZO10Nlh|
&& |lmDOpYhXR8SiFgP+8U9Qh8UyAL7pqygBwO7bB5+Ku6p4AFFv1jmZWU6A4E5XRbLIz1zYJu+6YAmPXtog5WQQwgjZbQxWoyyD7QaO3Q|
&& |aJloZMI3qUqzbyNEFIR6NAYvgzmrvRl8Z0qMUckgSpFo9IR65CYfALNCs7tGXatJcfWIOAcU6bGUBeU3YVJEvgzpSXXVQ8R5W9tUoU|
&& |3pDjxOeIREMPdqRAze+kK+DSHbXvw8kylewEVZvVvfVh0cCSrjCSNKB4kni2CHgNEYFfaEFBBZVRKgXKpaw4jSUgcLIXtizhQwo1dh|
&& |akIKKJNCgXLAkXFEIXzcC/ELYnWJY97KL+4wuncFoMLq6MAKAD2ikBjYozE3TqVGIoI4PTipKbR5JV8Kpn/7Oo05se4RPJhTmQkVhu|
&& |UeOl8CTnIv5z4QLm97EmtBHWFTCOD6pEaJcUJwojMRGyCLQet1Q3FYXQc10AjzaEsB1k6oN7cnCfmEAxfJyCcx9WQijCZN44/if4jG|
&& |3z7Wh39AZ/4F2/NP2HCTL6R/+zO+8LmYIvogpsi/JKYwxWZUPvanYrN/Icb1DHMFeqLx/rnCQ+m95+ZcnUkKOL5mzkXkqrmyk5MhkI|
&& |I9Gp+eu5pz6yJMOQAyg/dDOQBTLoVUOAg3+51cSv9sLuWx5N7P5qQeK/8gc3tEESbHSC7/3gC5PdI0QW7PVxt5PLdH5khLrKQgUOya|
&& |C8iRkvw35Uj3bK0IKsE/0ouuXOPNpZaRyQGDeqj0PJNrNuTsaS/1iZy9+r3axAcCgWHtg26MXkZfYVyAN6x9lA+Xpww6QY6pauL1Na|
&& |SYWEN6vDyOWouDEhNKN15fizsTa3FPFBH39JomJSyvr2kqPIM1zWfqzgxrw9RIeNmew4YVDor7uS4DWvnUBwDmKGvskWVYurX2ur+3|
&& |xi6HbOAl1CKsXdijrjWLWu79ARkbLcTtiK5VeK4MCaqklZoPk4Wtdae2wSEsLf0trPl4slRz0b6yUztDqgXApFBQFSaPCWjA3voEBV|
&& |A8pQbJoivOX6xBkmUYa5CernitNeug7+PhdHxQy0XQrwcEwwts5EWkck1cSic6XqiJK7XEwIvVzVptoa1LESC4mCOqUSlr5Qi16ZZe|
&& |x/gYUq3ez+BQLWM+zlCjWahCA/7UkLn6AGD4pVpXcClyRZEdnCLYouQXUOla38ArXduqsigfBEVYkuvHWtfXiu3l7S43qkHW7xXDvr|
&& |x7mMClW8ZGqBjmQjqony1f3FkBWinVXoOJrLHyun+x8prR6XSKWEPtNbH95kGA4Ai5LQfLzbXCdNxI5ftYwW5ZvILdj5PkclXfPE6X|
&& |rlBrb/GjT1XsyYCcNRco223HXmkQdwLkzcDeWk6wEwDHC3cCnGCHQsVaTxYmEBqthXsXa/Bo/uskop8A2anGMhb5Zy24o+KEMgZ7Ku|
&& |ioAkKlUwD0tvnZkndUnCsugSCddOXQowBzMwkHEwBLrFSxNHt7UwSjb0dtsYi7MpY2Qj/9VNhIYBbEvizadIH9NMo7wArK16ZH3aTg|
&& |roxN4bI5Pr5UGS7B4Vv6p+vShiBDKVOEWHE26o7MG4jdm+EV3GDUiucJfH5DVwiacr2TMBw1uwxzRiogOOslVPd9KOHtbAtDevIpgE|
&& |8U9iriLk2FO50WxwEg3lJmEcDZfiMbZTF5y35S3N4q74FU4khiBnWgKnjxS9uHcG/lw4mJD1DBp+97SUGN5KGl8p1EbRX65U1BcC+p|
&& |/7ZzNYB1d3eFe9glkrzJsCd3kiQA/PK2yIB7ch9aRHgQeEDBtrcZt4SINg2WHWJ19eAUKzEb8PUW7PG9ze/akcuRIpHnlS25KkiDrk|
&& |fIfNE6osSsNzxcJO/efHABmqDbXnvcYCrmCEAZ1GnELIfIEbjVVSD4Xvv6lZ1HuwhGtGb8zIJTKbMBKqEVUwhGU8jU4UBwa83PLDjm|
&& |n93mSiHHeJaf/QAJJNH66skaOWWE25jxgIr17Ifki04kqXhAwM/QAILvsWV0VAcbLNT9DI3NFK5naPRfdpZPME6cB866FTbbFKUjzi|
&& |LZnEyL76bbWSSXz0YRe7ArHgvAmS4uknsb4L0zXRo0heF2psv7DDeNIOeRgN94/MyKMx9Q3O5+1xrnLq5hyZX4djaO/3UHumxw15DS|
&& |alqU/r5FBR0kRhY3WxhwypZ2PWNoGr/lJCS7Wo+J8js+8ngAE5zVtEkkSCOe1bQe6xSX65ym/r5zr6qzPK9eKgclUjzzqtXOvErm7z|
&& |2ZqxrlOWw96GeHlTKFxfdSCHCaTppA75/BVv7AGWyINF/0fIV+J17y7zkyjMbvPxNwRdSO9NmKbLL7C85WvEM9o7L9XWdU/uEPf/jD|
&& |H34G/wGmG7NImfqvDAAAAABJRU5ErkJggg==|
&& |" alt="World" style="width:80px;" />|
&& | </div>|
&& | <p style="font-size:12px; color:#000;text-align:center">|
&& |   <b>HELLO WORLD!!!</b><br><br>|
&& | </p>|
&& | </div>|
&& |</body></html>|.


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 2: Extract all pictures and build new HTML Email Body
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
lv_html_out = lv_html_in.

" Get All Images...
FIND ALL OCCURRENCES OF REGEX `<img\b[^>]*>` IN lv_html_in RESULTS DATA(lt_matches) IGNORING CASE.
LOOP AT lt_matches ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<m>).
  DATA(lv_img) = lv_html_in+<m>-offset(<m>-length).

  " Get Source...
  FIND FIRST OCCURRENCE OF REGEX `src="([^"]+)"` IN lv_img SUBMATCHES DATA(lv_source) IGNORING CASE.
  IF sy-subrc = 0 AND lv_source IS NOT INITIAL.

    " BASE64...
    IF to_lower( lv_source ) CS ';base64,'.

      " Get Picture...
      FIND FIRST OCCURRENCE OF REGEX ';base64,(.*)$' IN lv_source SUBMATCHES DATA(lv_base64) IGNORING CASE.
      IF sy-subrc = 0 AND lv_base64 IS NOT INITIAL.

        ADD 1 TO lv_pic_cnt.

        " Get Picture Name...
        DATA(lv_pic_name) = |inline_image_{ lv_pic_cnt }.png|.

        " Append Image Table...
        CLEAR: lv_src.
        CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_BASE64_DECODE_STR'
          EXPORTING
            input  = lv_base64
          IMPORTING
            output = lv_src
          EXCEPTIONS
            failed = 1
            OTHERS = 2.
        IF sy-subrc <> 0.
          CONTINUE.
        ENDIF.
        " Mime type is hardcoded here for simplicity...
        APPEND VALUE #( name = lv_pic_name
                        mime = 'image/png'
                        src=lv_src  ) TO lt_pic_tab[].

        " Build new Source...
        DATA(lv_new_source) = |cid:{ lv_pic_name }|.

        " Replace Source...
        DATA(lv_new_img) = lv_img.
        REPLACE FIRST OCCURRENCE OF lv_source IN lv_new_img WITH lv_new_source.

        " Replace Image...
        REPLACE FIRST OCCURRENCE OF lv_img IN lv_html_out WITH lv_new_img.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 3: Convert HTML-Out STRING to SOLIX
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
lt_html_out[] = cl_document_bcs=>string_to_soli( lv_html_out ).


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 4: Create MIME Multi-Part Object for Email
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE OBJECT lo_mime_srv.


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 5: Add Inline Images (Content-ID Based Embedding)
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
LOOP AT lt_pic_tab ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_pic>).
  CLEAR: lt_solix[], lv_len.
  lt_solix = cl_bcs_convert=>xstring_to_solix( <fs_pic>-src ).
  lv_len   = xstrlen( <fs_pic>-src ).

  lo_mime_srv->add_binary_part(
    content      = lt_solix
    content_type = <fs_pic>-mime
    length       = lv_len
    content_id   = <fs_pic>-name ).
ENDLOOP.


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 6: Set HTML Body into MIME Service
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
lo_mime_srv->set_main_html(
  content     = lt_html_out
  filename    = 'email.htm'
  description = 'Hello World!' ).

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 7: Create Email Document with Subject
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
TRY.
    lo_doc = cl_document_bcs=>create_from_multirelated(
      i_subject          = 'Hello World!'
      i_multirel_service = lo_mime_srv ).
  CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_root).
    WRITE: /, |Something went wrong....|. RETURN.
ENDTRY.

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 8: Create and Send Email Using BCS
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
TRY.
    lo_bcs = cl_bcs=>create_persistent( ).
    lo_bcs->set_document( lo_doc ).

    lo_sender = cl_sapuser_bcs=>create( sy-uname ).
    lo_bcs->set_sender( lo_sender ).

    lo_recipient = cl_cam_address_bcs=>create_internet_address(
                     'hello.world@gmail.com' ).
    lo_bcs->add_recipient( lo_recipient ).

    lo_bcs->send( i_with_error_screen = abap_true ).

  CATCH cx_root INTO lx_root.
    WRITE: /, |Something went wrong....|. RETURN.
ENDTRY.

COMMIT WORK AND WAIT.

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Final Message
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
WRITE / 'Email sent successfully Check It in SOST. !'.

 Output: 

Explanation

The program performs the following major steps:

  1. Construct an HTML template containing inline Base64‑encoded images. (But you may get it as local file on your PC or form MIME repository. These options are not considered here ).
  2. Scan the HTML for <img> tags and extract the Base64 image data. 
  3. Decode each Base64 image with the help of function module SCMS_BASE64_DECODE_STRE and store it as a binary part for the email. 
  4. Replace Base64 data in HTML with CID references (e.g., cid:inline_image_1.png).
  5. Convert HTML to BCS format (SO_DOCUMENT format).
  6. Create a MIME multi-part message via class CL_GBT_MULTIRELATED_SERVICE.
  7. Embed all images as MIME parts so email clients can render them inline.
  8. Assemble the email document with subject, HTML body, and images.
  9. Send the email using BCS to the configured recipient.

This ABAP report builds an HTML email containing Base64‑encoded inline images, extracts and decodes those images, embeds them as MIME parts in the email, rewrites the HTML to use Content‑ID (CID) references, and sends the fully constructed HTML email with inline images using the SAP BCS framework. The email is sent from the logged-on SAP user to a fixed email address and can be traced via transaction SOST.

 

Thank you for taking the time to read my post. I hope this information helps anyone facing a similar requirement. If you have questions, feedback, or suggestions for improvement, feel free to leave a comment below.

entry.title: Custom Business Configurations (F4579): Define default grouping and sorting
entry.content:
Introduction

The Custom Business Configurations (CUBCO) app serves as an entry point to the Business Configuration Maintenance Object (SMBC) provided by custom applications or partners.

This blog explains how to define the default grouping and sorting for the tables of the business configuration object.
The basic procedure is already explained here, but we apply it directly to the result of the ADT wizard.

This blog is relevant for

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
  • SAP BTP ABAP environment 
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 

Further reading:

  • Related blog posts
  • Learn how you can use ABAP technology to develop innovative applications and business solutions across SAP’s portfolio on SAP Learning Site.

Example scenario

Company codes are defined in the configuration table ZDEMO_CC. In the CUBCO app, the rows are to be grouped and sorted by country.

@EndUserText.label : 'Company Code'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zdemo_cc {
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key company_code      : abap.numc(4) not null;
  country               : land1;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

Define presentation variant

First you use the ADT wizard to generate a Business Configuration Maintenance Object for this table.

In the generated CDS view ZI_COMPANYCODE_S of the Singleton entity, you change the facet type from #LINEITEM_REFERENCE to #PRESENTATIONVARIANT_REFERENCE and add the targetQualifier attribute:

  @ui.facet: [ {
    id: 'ZI_CompanyCode', 
    purpose: #STANDARD, 
    type: #PRESENTATIONVARIANT_REFERENCE, 
    targetQualifier: 'myVariant',
    label: 'Company Codes', 
    position: 1 , 
    targetElement: '_CompanyCode'
  } ]
  .lineItem: [ {
    position: 1 
  } ]
  key 1 as SingletonID,

In the metadata extension ZI_COMPANYCODE of the company code entity, add the following presentation variant:

@ui.presentationVariant: [
  {
    qualifier: 'myVariant',
    sortOrder: [
      {
        by: 'Country',
        direction: #ASC
      }
    ],
    groupBy: [ 'Country' ],
    visualizations: [{type: #AS_LINEITEM}]
  }
]
annotate view ZI_CompanyCode with

Finally, open the generated SMBC object ZCOMPANYCODE in ADT and open the table settings section. Change the table type attribute from Grid Table to Responsive Table because grouping is not supported for grid tables. If you only need the default sorting, you can continue to use the grid table.

entry.title: Our 2026 Roadmap for Joule for Developers ABAP AI capabilities
entry.content:
Sapphire 2026 is approaching quickly and as usual it will bring lots of new announcements. So now is the right point in time to recap what has been announced already until Q2/2026.

The ABAP Platform AI (covering both SAP BTP ABAP Environment and SAP S/4HANA) transitions from a set of independent AI skills to full-scale Agentic AI. The roadmap focuses heavily on developer productivity via Joule and the modernization of ABAP development tools. The key benefits for customers and partners of the roadmap until Q2/2026 are:

 

  1. ABAP development tools in VS Code with ABAP MCP Server.
  2. ABAP AI for lower releases of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (i.e. 2021, 2022, 2023 and 2025).
  3. Simplified customer on-boarding of ABAP AI via SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration.
  4. Faster innovation with Side-by-Side consumption of SAP and 3rd party Agentic IDEs and LLMs
    (e.g. Microsoft, Amazon, IBM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral…)
 

The biggest shift in 2026 will be the move toward Agentic AI, where the existing ABAP AI skills doesn't just explain, complete or generate code (e.g. RAP, CDS, Tests) independently but acts as an autonomous collaborator. This includes built-in ABAP MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. The introduction of VS Code as IDE can only be a start as the depth and breath of Eclipse which we achieved in so many years cannot be reached with one or two steps in VS Code. It'll be a journey which starts with first providing End-to-end UI services development with agents.

The new ABAP AI side-by-side architecture is designed to provide a scalable and future‑proof foundation for ABAP AI capabilities across heterogeneous SAP landscapes. It enables customers to consume ABAP AI services side‑by‑side, independent of the underlying S/4HANA release, ensuring access even for lower‑release Private Edition systems. 

A core benefit of the architecture is also its simplified enablement model through SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration, reducing on-boarding effort and ensuring consistent lifecycle management across environments. 
In addition, the architecture allows us to accelerate innovation speed by allowing to leverage SAP‑provided ABAP AI models alongside leading third‑party LLMs (e.g., Microsoft, Amazon, IBM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral) more independently from releases. This flexibility ensures rapid adoption of new capabilities without dependency on individual release cycles.

If you missed the highlights we delivered in Q1/2026 have a look into what Olga Dolinskaja shared about ABAP AI ATC finding explain and custom code explain and proposals. Although this will be a focus also for our future roadmap we will also not stop investing into AI app generators like the Embedded Analytics model generator. 

 
Here you can learn more:
  1. SAP Joule for Developers (J4D) Expands to Private Cloud: Accelerating ABAP Innovation and Transformation
  2. Your Roadmap for getting started with ABAP AI
  3. ABAP AI Revolution Accelerates: The ABAP Developer Who Built Enterprise Apps in Minutes
  4. Joule for Developers and ABAP AI capabilities are coming to SAP S/4HANA Private Edition 2021, 2022, and 2023
  5. Public Roadmap: SAP Help / SAP Roadmap Explorer
  6. Help & Guides
    1. Step-by-step guide how to activate SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
    2. Joule for Developers (J4D) for S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup Guide
    3. SAP ABAP AI Discovery Center
    4. SAP BTP Discovery Center (Free Tier Services)
    5. Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
    6. YouTube playlist for Joule for Developers (J4D)
    7. Joule for Developers Help (J4D)
    8. Build SAP Fiori Apps with ABAP Cloud and SAP Joule for developers (RAP120) 
    9. ADT Eclipse AI Capabilities Help
    10. ABAP AI SDK Help
    11. ABAP-1 Help

More high-level insights about Joule for Developers/ABAP AI and ABAP platform can be found here . Here you find more on ABAP- 1,

entry.title: Optional Task: Use Joule CDS Unit Test Generation
entry.content:
Thank you to everyone who participated in the ABAP Developer Challenge over the past four weeks and successfully completed all four tasks focused on ABAP AI capabilities. We truly appreciate the enthusiasm and engagement shown throughout the challenge. As a follow-up, we are excited to introduce an additional bonus task.

In Week 2, you enhanced a CDS data definition by implementing simple logic. Building on that work, your next step is to create an ABAP test class to validate the logic within the CDS data definition. For this task, you will utilize another feature of Joule’s ABAP AI capabilities — CDS Test Generation.

Task:

  • Select the CDS data definition that you enhanced in Week 2.
  • Generate an ABAP test class using Joule’s CDS Test Generation feature.
  • Execute the test class and ensure that all generated test cases run successfully.

Please refer to the link below for detailed guidance on how to use this ABAP AI capability.

https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-development-tools-user-guide/use-cds-test-class-generator?locale=en-US

entry.title: Smaller ABAP Runtime Size Now Available in SAP BTP ABAP Environment
entry.content:
Starting March 31, 2026, SAP BTP ABAP environment introduces a new runtime sizing option: 0.5 ABAP Compute Units (ACU) which is 8 GB of runtime memory.

With the introduction of 0.5 ACU, customers can now operate ABAP systems at 50% lower ABAP runtime cost compared to the previous minimum size of 1 ACU making it significantly more cost-efficient to run development, test, and sandbox systems.

This enhancement allows SAP BTP ABAP environment to run on smaller runtime units,  lowering the entry barrier for development and test systems.

What is an ABAP Compute Unit (ACU)?

ABAP Compute Units (ACUs) define the runtime capacity of an SAP BTP ABAP environment system. One ACU corresponds to 16 GB of memory allocated to the ABAP runtime. The total runtime size determines how much capacity is available across all ABAP application servers. For more information, see the documentation on ABAP Compute Units.

What’s New?

With this update SAP BTP ABAP environment now supports 0.5 ABAP Compute Units (ACU) as the new minimum system configuration.

It allows to run smaller systems, enabling more granular runtime sizing compared to previous configurations. It creates an additional option to better match the actual workloads, particularly for smaller environments.

What are the Key Benefits?

  • 50% lower ABAP runtime cost compared to the previous minimum size (1 ACU)
  • Improved resource utilization for smaller or variable workloads
  • Lower entry barrier for new and existing customers

For a detailed breakdown of cost components, see my previous blog post on ABAP environment cost calculation.

How to Adjust Existing Systems?

Adjusting the ABAP runtime size (ACUs) is handled via manual scaling and does not lead to a system downtime . You can use the SAP BTP Cockpit to update the service instance, for example, from 1 ACU to 0,5 ACU, see the documentation on Updating an ABAP System.

What Needs to be Considered?

For systems with a runtime size of 0.5 ACU, only one application server is used. This setup is ideal for development and test, but not recommended for productive use, as it may increase the likelihood of short outages.

Overview about typical use cases and recommend systems sizes

Use Case

Size

Total Runtime MemoryApplication Servers

Development, test, sandbox systems for smaller projects

0.5 ACU (new)

8 GB1 á 8 GB
Development, test, sandbox systems for bigger projects;
Productive systems for up to 1,000 active users per day		1 ACU16 GB2 á 8 GB
Productive systems with higher concurrency or up to 100 tenants 2 ACU32 GB4 á 8 GB
Productive systems with bigger max session size4 ACU64 GB2 á 32 GB
Productive systems with higher load in multitenant SaaS solution 4 ACU64 GB8 á 8 GB

 

Further References 

For more information about sizing options and configuration parameters in SAP BTP ABAP environment, see the documentation on Creating an ABAP System.

entry.title: The Agentic Revolution Is Here — And Your ABAP Code Is the Foundation
entry.content:
Three Waves in Two Years

To understand where we are, you need to understand the three inflection points Jensen described. They happened faster than anyone expected.

Wave 1: Generative AI (2022–2023)

ChatGPT changed computing from retrieval-based to generative. Instead of a database returning a record, a model generates an answer. Jensen put it bluntly: "Computing used to be retrieval-based. Now it's generative." The architecture implications of that sentence are enormous and still rippling through every layer of enterprise software.

Wave 2: Reasoning AI (2024)

OpenAI's o1 model introduced something that made generative AI trustworthy: the ability to think. To reflect. To decompose a problem it couldn't solve into steps it could. To ground itself on facts before answering. Reasoning AI made large language models genuinely useful for complex, multi-step work — not just creative writing and summarisation.

Wave 3: Agentic AI (2025–2026)

This is the one that changes your job. Jensen cited Claude Code — the very AI assistant many of us now use daily — as the breakthrough moment:

"Claude Code has revolutionised software engineering. There is not one software engineer at NVIDIA today who is not assisted by an AI coding agent."

The shift is fundamental. With generative AI, you ask a question. With agentic AI, you give a task. The agent reads your files, writes code, compiles it, tests it, finds the failures, fixes them, and iterates — autonomously, in a loop, until the work is done or it needs your input.

Computing demand, Jensen said, has grown one million times in the last two years. That is not a typo. The explosion is driven by agentic inference: every time an agent thinks, plans, reads a file, calls a tool, or generates an output, it burns tokens.

GTC 2026 Keynote — Jensen Huang's enterprise agentic architectures slide (02:02:57). SAP is bottom-left. Each diagram is a production blueprint, not a roadmap.

 

What Jensen Showed at 02:02:57

At just over two hours into the keynote, Jensen showed a slide he called a renaissance. Each of the twelve architecture diagrams was a real, production blueprint. Not a roadmap. Not a vision. Each company had already rebuilt its product around AI agents.

"This is a reinvention. A renaissance of enterprise IT. Every single SaaS company will become an Agentic as a Service company. No question about it."

He compared it to three previous platform shifts:

  • When Linux arrived → every company needed a Linux strategy
  • When HTML/HTTP arrived → every company needed a web strategy
  • When Kubernetes arrived → every company needed a cloud-native strategy
  • Now Now OpenClaw (the open-source agentic OS) has arrived → every company needs an agentic strategy

NemoClaw and SAP: A Security Partnership

Jensen announced NemoClaw as NVIDIA's enterprise-hardened version of OpenClaw. To understand where it fits in the SAP picture, it helps to be precise about what it is — and what it is not.

OpenClaw is, in Jensen's words, "the operating system of agentic computers." It handles task decomposition, sub-agent spawning, tool calling, file access, cron scheduling, multi-modal I/O, and LLM orchestration. It reached more GitHub stars in a few weeks than Linux achieved in thirty years.

The enterprise problem with a system that can "access sensitive information, execute code, and communicate externally" is obvious. You cannot deploy raw OpenClaw in an SAP landscape and have it reach your vendor master, financial postings, and HR records without controls.

NemoClaw solves this by adding three security layers on top of OpenClaw:

Layer

What It Does

OpenShell

Sandboxes agent execution; controls what it can read, write, and call

Policy Engine

Enforces the enterprise's own business rules

Network Guardrail
+ Privacy Router

Prevents data leaving the enterprise boundary

 

Important clarification: NemoClaw is not the foundation SAP Joule is built on. Joule is SAP's own product, with its own architecture, models, and AI infrastructure running on BTP and SAP AI Core. NemoClaw is NVIDIA technology that SAP — as a launch enterprise partner — is integrating as an optional security and governance layer within BTP, tied specifically to Joule Studio. It is available for customers who require the strongest possible compliance controls when agents interact with sensitive ERP, financial, or supply chain data.

The architecture looks like this:

The bottom line for ABAP developers remains unchanged regardless of which security envelope is in play: when an agent needs to do something in SAP — read a purchase order, create a vendor, trigger a workflow, query a cost centre — it calls your ABAP.

Your BAPIs. Your CDS views. Your OData services.

 

What This Means in Practice

Let me make this concrete with an example that exists today.

A procurement manager asks Joule: "Which of our top twenty suppliers have delivery performance below 90% this quarter, and what is the total open invoice value against them?"

In the pre-agentic world, someone writes a report in SE38, schedules it, exports to Excel, and manually cross-references two tables. Elapsed time: hours, or days if it hits a queue.

In the agentic world, Joule:

  1. Decomposes the question into sub-tasks
  2. Calls a procurement agent → queries I_SupplierDeliveryPerformance CDS view via OData
  3. Calls a finance agent → queries I_SupplierOpenItemMD CDS view via OData
  4. Synthesises both results
  5. Returns a structured answer in seconds

The entire chain runs through NemoClaw's policy engine and your ABAP authorization framework. The agent's service user has only the authorizations you have granted. Every CDS view it accesses must have the proper access control annotation and a corresponding DCL.

 

The Four Things Your ABAP Must Get Right
If agents are going to call your code — and they are — four things matter more than they ever did before.


1. Clean, Annotated CDS Views


CDS views are the primary structured data interface for AI agents in SAP. An agent does not navigate transaction codes. It calls OData endpoints backed by CDS. If your CDS views lack semantic annotations, have incomplete access control, or expose data structures that predate any logical data model, they become a liability.The minimum bar for agent-ready CDS:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'Supplier Delivery Performance'
@VDM.viewType: #CONSUMPTION
@OData.publish: true
define view entity I_SupplierDeliveryPerformance
  as select from ...
{
  @Semantics.businessPartner.role: #SUPPLIER
  SupplierID,

  @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure: 'DeliveryUnit'
  ConfirmedDeliveryQty,

  @Semantics.percentage
  OnTimeDeliveryRate
}

Semantic annotations tell the LLM what each field means. Without them, the agent is guessing from column names. With them, it can reason correctly.

 

2. Authority Checks on Every Agent-Callable Interface


NemoClaw's policy engine delegates final security enforcement to ABAP. This is not a weakness — it is the correct architecture. The LLM should not be trusted with authorization decisions. ABAP should.
Every function module, BAPI, and method that an agent can call must perform an explicit authority check:

METHOD get_supplier_invoice_data.
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_LFA1_BUK'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD iv_company_code
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_sy_authorization_check
      EXPORTING
        textid = cx_sy_authorization_check=>no_authorization.
  ENDIF.

  " ... proceed with data retrieval
ENDMETHOD.

An agent running as a service user with overly broad authorizations is an insider threat waiting to happen. Lock it down at the ABAP layer.

3. Machine-Speed Performance


A human clicks a button every thirty seconds. An agent may call your BAPI ten thousand times per hour. An interface that performs acceptably under human load may collapse completely under agent load.
Review your agent-callable interfaces against these criteria:
• No SELECT * in performance-critical paths — agents fetch data in loops, field-level selects only.
• Buffer vendor master, material master, and configuration data where appropriate.
• Avoid dialogue modules and unnecessary commits in agent-exposed interfaces — agents expect atomic, stateless calls.
• Consider ABAP Cloud compatibility — if you are on S/4HANA, agent integrations will increasingly come via BTP.


4. Reliable, Structured Error Returns


Agents chain multiple tool calls into a single business process. If step three of a five-step chain fails silently — no exception raised, just a blank result set — the agent may proceed with corrupted state and complete a transaction based on missing data.
Your interfaces need to fail loudly, clearly, and catchably:

METHOD create_purchase_requisition.
TRY.
" ... BAPI call
IF lt_return[] IS NOT INITIAL.
LOOP AT lt_return INTO ls_return
WHERE type = 'E' OR type = 'A'.
RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_pr_creation_failed
EXPORTING
message = ls_return-message
message_id = ls_return-id
number = ls_return-number.
ENDLOOP.
ENDIF.
CATCH cx_sy_authorization_check INTO DATA(lx_auth).
RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_pr_creation_failed
EXPORTING message = 'Authorization check failed'.
ENDTRY.
ENDMETHOD.

A well-structured exception gives the orchestration layer the information it needs to retry, compensate, or escalate. A dump gives it nothing.

 

Physical AI: Why EWM and IBP Developers Should Pay Close Attention

Jensen spent a significant portion of the keynote on physical AI — robots, autonomous vehicles, digital twins. At first glance this might seem distant from ABAP.  It is not.

He announced partnerships with ABB, KUKA, Universal Robots, BYD, Hyundai, Nissan, and Geely — collectively representing the automation of manufacturing floors and logistics networks at a scale never seen before. These physical systems will generate transactions. Inventory movements, goods receipts, production confirmations, delivery completions — triggered by machines at machine speed, not by warehouse workers scanning barcodes.

If you work in SAP Extended Warehouse Management, SAP Integrated Business Planning, or SAP Manufacturing Integration and Intelligence, the interfaces you build today will be called by robots tomorrow. The same four principles apply — and the performance requirement becomes even more acute.

 

Your Learning Roadmap

The field is moving fast. But not everything needs to be learned at once. Here is how I would prioritise it.

Start Now — Zero Extra Investment Required

  • AI tools today. Use AI coding tools on real ABAP work — every unit test, every code review, every CDS view.
  • Learn what SAP Joule can already do. Log into your BTP account and explore the Joule capabilities available today.
  • Audit your CDS views. Run a quick check across your landscape for CDS views with @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED — these are potential agent-accessible data leaks.

Next Three to Six Months

  • ABAP RAP (RESTful Application Programming Model). If you have been deferring it, stop. RAP is the model that produces the OData V4 services that Joule agents consume.
  • SAP AI Core and SAP BTP basics. You do not need to become a BTP developer. Understand the platform well enough to design ABAP interfaces that integrate cleanly with it.
  • Python literacy. Not fluency — literacy. Most AI tooling and agent orchestration frameworks are Python-first. Being able to read a Python agent script is now a professional responsibility.

Strategic Awareness

  • OpenClaw and MCP (Model Context Protocol). OpenClaw is the open-source agent OS. MCP is the protocol that defines how agents discover and call tools. SAP Joule implements MCP under the hood.
  • HANA Vector Engine. HANA Cloud's vector engine brings unstructured data (PDFs, emails, contracts) directly into your SAP data layer.

 

The Part Nobody Is Saying Loudly Enough

Jensen made a prediction that deserves more attention than it received.

In the near future, every engineer at NVIDIA will receive an annual token budget — roughly equivalent to half their salary in additional compensation — because AI-amplified engineers are demonstrably ten times more productive. Engineers are already asking: "How many tokens come with this job?"

Here is the part that matters for us specifically:

AI tokens without SAP domain expertise produce plausible-looking but wrong ABAP. An LLM that does not understand the difference between a financial posting document and a parked document will generate code that compiles, passes basic tests, and silently corrupts your general ledger. It will call RFC_READ_TABLE in production because it found an example online. It will bypass the credit check in a sales order because it did not know the check existed.

Your twenty years of knowing where the bodies are buried in SAP — that is not a liability in an AI-augmented world. It is the irreplaceable ingredient that makes the AI's output correct and trustworthy.

The developers who will thrive are not the ones who resist this shift. And they are not the ones who hand everything to an LLM and hope for the best. They are the ones who treat AI as a powerful amplifier of their own judgment — who know enough to direct it well, verify its output, and design the systems it integrates with.

That combination — deep SAP domain knowledge plus AI tool fluency — is genuinely rare right now. The window to build it is open. It will not stay open long.

 

Wrapping Up

GTC 2026 was not a technology conference for GPU enthusiasts. It was a signal that the enterprise software industry has passed an inflection point. Jensen's slide showing SAP, Salesforce, ServiceNow, and Siemens side by side — each with a production agentic architecture — was not a prediction of what is coming. It was a photograph of what has already happened.

SAP has placed its bet. Joule is real, BTP is the platform, and the structured data of your SAP landscape — managed, modelled, and secured by ABAP code — is what gives every agent running on top of it the ground truth it needs to be useful.

Action Items

✓  Write clean CDS views.

✓  Lock down your authority checks.

✓  Tune for machine-speed consumption.

✓  Raise clear exceptions.

✓  Learn RAP.

✓  Open Claude Code.

  

The revolution is not coming for your job. It is coming for the parts of your job that should have been automated years ago. What remains — and what becomes more valuable — is everything that requires judgment, domain knowledge, and the ability to see when the AI is confidently wrong.

That is the ABAP developer of 2026.
